# XAIP — Signed Execution Receipts for AI Agent Tool Calls

> XAIP is a provider-neutral, open protocol for recording co-signed, hash-only
> execution receipts of AI agent tool calls — each signed by both the executing
> agent and the caller, so neither side can unilaterally fabricate one. It lets
> an agent inspect a tool,
> skill, or agent's prior execution evidence before delegating work or
> authorizing payment. Receipts are the primary artifact; trust scores are one
> derived view over them.

## The idea

Evidence before delegation. Most agent tool selection today is blind: the agent
sees a name and a description, not how the option behaved before. XAIP makes
behavior-derived execution evidence available before delegation. A single
operator can use its own receipt history as a local reliability signal today;
the same signed receipts become portable, independently verifiable evidence the
moment a second party reads them — so adoption does not depend on a network
forming first.

## How it works

- Each tool call is recorded as a receipt the caller co-signs alongside the
  executing agent — dual Ed25519 signatures over the same JCS-canonical payload,
  so neither party can unilaterally fabricate it. Co-signing is recommended, not
  required: a receipt may also carry the executor signature alone. Only hashes of
  the task and result are stored — no raw input or output content leaves the machine.
- The signed payload is canonicalized with JCS (RFC 8785) so two parties who saw
  the same payload agree on its hash.
- Receipts are portable across MCP, LangChain.js, OpenAI-compatible tool-call
  loops, and other runtimes.
- A public Trust API derives observed success probability and risk flags from
  the receipt graph, queryable without an account.
- `precheck()` in the SDK returns the available execution evidence for a set of
  candidate tools before an agent delegates to any of them.

## Key pages

- Live dashboard: https://xkumakichi.github.io/xaip-protocol/
- Evidence Before Delegation (one-screen live demo): https://xkumakichi.github.io/xaip-protocol/evidence-before-delegation.html
- Before Payment Evidence (seeded demo): https://xkumakichi.github.io/xaip-protocol/before-payment-demo.html
- Browser playground: https://xkumakichi.github.io/xaip-protocol/playground.html

## Quick start

- Fastest path to a first signed receipt (~5 minutes, measured): install the
  Claude Code hook (`npm install -g xaip-claude-hook && xaip-claude-hook install`);
  every MCP tool call in a normal session then emits a co-signed wire format v1
  receipt (hash-only). Steps with real outputs:
  https://github.com/xkumakichi/xaip-protocol#quick-start--your-first-signed-receipt-in-under-5-minutes

## Spec and implementation

- IETF Internet-Draft (individual submission, no formal IETF standing): https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-xkumakichi-xaip-receipts/
- Executable conformance test vectors (wire formatVersion "1": real hashes, canonical payloads, Ed25519 signatures; dependency-free checker): https://github.com/xkumakichi/xaip-protocol/tree/main/docs/spec/test-vectors
- Source code (MIT): https://github.com/xkumakichi/xaip-protocol
- npm packages: xaip-sdk, xaip-claude-hook, xaip-langchain, xaip-openai

## Writing

- "The agent-payment stack is taking shape. Portable execution evidence is still missing." — defines the pre-delegation execution-evidence design problem: subject of the evidence, unit (per-call vs session), assertion semantics, content vs hash, signing models, independent verification, selective disclosure, and the separation of evidence from decision policy. https://github.com/xkumakichi/xaip-protocol/blob/main/docs/articles/03-evidence-before-payment.md

## Relationship to adjacent work

XAIP records per-call execution receipts. This is complementary to session-level
audit-bundle formats (for example, the work of the W3C AIVS Community Group):
per-call execution evidence and session-level provenance sit at adjacent layers
rather than competing.

## What XAIP is not

XAIP is not a sandbox, not an approval engine, and not a payment rail. It does
not make tools safe and does not guarantee trust. It makes execution evidence
visible before delegation.
